title: "Psychocats Tutorials Updates"

forum: The Cafe

### Post by aysiu on 2007-04-21
**For New Users:**
[The Psychocats site](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/) is a great site for new users to explore. It uses a combination of terminal commands and screenshot-heavy point-and-click tutorials to help get you up and running with Ubuntu, and it addresses many common questions and problems newcomers have when starting with Ubuntu.

Psychocats isn't intended to compete with other documentation sites. Its goal is to give a uniform look and feel for several key tutorials and, more importantly, not to overwhelm or confuse new users with too many tutorials in hard-to-find places. Each tutorial addresses a common Ubuntu question, and all the internal links are on the sidebar.

If you're interested in a more comprehensive set of tutorials, especially ones aimed at advanced or intermediate use (or just anything not covered on my site--for example, how to use *ndiswrapper*), you may want to check out [the Tutorials & Tips section of these forums,](http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=100) or [the official community-maintained documentation](https://help.ubuntu.com/community).

**For Other Users:**
Thank you for all your help so far!

Users on these forums have caught typos and other errors and kept me abreast of changes in versions of software I don't use (e.g. Songbird). Some users have even contributed whole paragraphs to the Psychocats tutorials, and I've tried to credit them appropriately.

**Working with Psychocats:**
I've had numerous requests asking if people can link to or translate the Psychocats Ubuntu website. The answer is *yes*. You can link to Psychocats without asking my permission. If you want to translate Psychocats Ubuntu, you may do that as well (please let me know, though--I'm just curious to know what's going on), and I encourage spreading the knowledge. I haven't officially licensed the documentation, but the closest I've found to what I'd say embodies the spirit with which I'm giving Psychocats Ubuntu to the community is [the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 license ](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/3.0/). If you would like to mirror Psychocats, you may do so, but please send me a link to the mirrored site, so I can know to refer people there also.

**Future Updates and Feedback:**
If you have any constructive criticism, please leave it in this thread. I'll be checking back from time to time. I devote a lot of time to the Ubuntu community and to these forums, especially, but this is not my main job. So go ahead and leave any suggestions you may have, but I may not get around to implementing them right away (or at all, depending on how good the suggestion is).

If the Psychocats website has helped you, you can post that here, too. It's always good to know what people have found helpful, and which pages are most frequently used by people.

### Post by saulgoode on 2007-04-21
The tutorial for obtaining Adobe/Macromedia's Flashplayer bypasses the mechanism whereby the user is presented with the terms of its [End User Licensing Agreement](http://www.adobe.com/products/eulas/players/flash/). Perhaps the installation process offers the opportunity to accept or reject those terms but Adobe's website suggests that the EULA is accepted by the act of downloading. 

Since some may find the terms of the EULA to be unacceptable (particularly businesses or "organizations"), it would probably be safest if a link to the EULA's terms were mentioned in the tutorial before downloading takes place.

### Post by aysiu on 2007-04-22

I'm still not 100% sure I know what you're talking about. Are you saying you'd copy a command like ```
sudo apt-get update
``` and paste that in the terminal and it'd work fine? Then you'd try to copy and paste ```
sudo apt-get dist-upgrade
``` but instead of *dist-upgrade* appearing, it'd paste *update* again?

### Post by aysiu on 2007-05-13
OrangeCrate, glad to hear it. I don't get any monetary compensation for creating Psychocats, so knowing that it actually helps people means a great deal to me.

**New Changes**: I've added a new page to Psychocats called [Which *buntu to pick?](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/whichbuntu). It answers questions about the differences between Ubuntu, Kubuntu, Xubuntu, Edubuntu; 7.04, 6.10, 6.06, 5.10; DVD, CD; Desktop, Server, Alternate; and more.

I've also modified [the ISO page](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/iso) to include more links to other tutorials, which may, at this point, be better than the current ISO page. In theory, I'd like to update the ISO page, but in reality it's probably not going to happen any time soon.

I've also included a link to [Is Ubuntu for You?](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=63315).

I'm planning very soon (in the next week or two) to include a new [installing page](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/installing) for Feisty Fawn. Stay tuned!

### Post by picpak on 2007-06-06
Some comments about your [Install Internet Explorer](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/ies4linux) page:

1) The guide says to type in ies4linux in Firefox and you'll be taken to the page. It takes you to an ad page.

2) It also says to scroll down until you see the Download link, but the Download link is at the top.

3) The line

```
sudo mv ~/bin/ie6 /usr/local/bin/ie6 rmdir ~/bin
```

should have an && in it, as in:

```
sudo mv ~/bin/ie6 /usr/local/bin/ie6 && rmdir ~/bin
```

That's all!

---

### Post by Rinzwind on 2007-11-09
Excellent install how-to. The images make it impossible ask questions about it  :D

You might save alot of room by having 2 or 3 of those screens next to eachother and group the text for those 

Or have the text on top and all the images underneath them. This will show all the needed packages (icewm icewm-common and icwm-themes) at 1 glance. Plus the more experienced user will have enough with knowing the names of the packages.

### Post by aysiu on 2007-11-09
> **Rinzwind said:**
> Excellent install how-to. The images make it impossible ask questions about it  :D

You might save alot of room by having 2 or 3 of those screens next to eachother and group the text for those 

Or have the text on top and all the images underneath them. This will show all the needed packages (icewm icewm-common and icwm-themes) at 1 glance. Plus the more experienced user will have enough with knowing the names of the packages.
I am trying to make it as step-by-step as possible. I feel there's enough documentation out there for IceWM that assumes all you need to know are the package names. This one is targeted specifically toward the uninitiated user--one who is just beginning to feel comfortable with Linux and Gnome but who may want to explore something else.

If there is enough of a demand for it, I can also create a much shorter, text-only guide to IceWM for the slightly more advanced users.

### Post by aysiu on 2007-11-09
> **K.Mandla said:**
> Thanks for that. I sometimes tinker with IceWM but get frustrated and throw it out, because I can't ever get the wallpaper to change. :evil: Sigh. ...
Well, I've added a few tweaks to the tutorial.

As far as changing the wallpaper goes, it's a little tricky, because it can be a three-part process.

1. You have to specify in the ~/.icewm/preferences file the location of the wallpaper image
2. You have to run the command ```
icewmbg
```
3. You have to make sure the theme you're using doesn't specify a wallpaper in the theme.default file, especially if it's specifying a wallpaper file that doesn't exist

Hope that helps!

---

### Post by aysiu on 2008-05-02
Okay, it's taken me several days... almost a week, but I think I have the bulk of Psychocats updated for Ubuntu 8.04. I may do a proofread in a few days and just make sure there aren't holes I've missed. Eventually, I'd like to update the PartImage tutorial so it isn't using Hoary Hedgehog screenshots.

The biggest change is probably to the page on installing software. I thought my old page was a little too text-heavy for beginners, and I really wanted to point people in the direction of using the easy ways to install software and using the more difficult ways as a last resort, and include as little explanation as possible without neglecting essential information.

More changes to come, but they'll come slowly.

---

### Post by DonnieP on 2008-06-30
A note about
[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/nonautilusplease]("http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/nonautilusplease")

I had installed LXDE to see what it was like.  Its default file manager is pcmanfm.  When I went back to Gnome, pcmanfm was still the default, instead of nautilus.  I tried the psychocat script to restore Nautilus, but it didn't do the job for me.  After mucking around in the pcmanfm man files I discovered /usr/share/applications/mimeinfo.cache.  Removing all references there to pcmanfm.desktop did the trick.

### Post by boddhisatva on 2008-08-16
The site seems to be very helpful, however I think there should be some warnings when suggesting copying/pasting some code for seemingly innocent operations, especially as it's aimed at inexperienced users. I used the code in the 'pure gnome' section to remove my kubuntu desktop and then experienced some moments of sheer terror as I saw half of my carefully accumulated software collection disappear. Why are programs such as Blender, VLC, Audacity (to mention but a few) removed - they are not specifically KDE? I lost most of my multimedia collection. Well, now I just have to search all the repositories and websites for all the programs, hoping I'll remember all the names.
Sorry for this sour note, I appreciate all the unpaid effort of people in the open source community, but you can understand my frustration. Perhaps I should have just 'played it safe' and left the unused KDE desktop alone instead of experimenting.

---

### Post by lukjad on 2008-08-16
The reasons why they were removed (I think) is that they were dependent on the KDE. With KDE gone, they weren't usable so they were removed too. 
I could be wrong but that is my understanding.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2008-08-16
Removing an entire desktop environment isn't any less simple than reinstalling VLC or Audacity is.

The fact is that if packages are removed, they can just as easily be reinstalled, and none of your settings will be gone.

What kind of warning would be suitable? You really can't know what's going to happen until you run the command, and I don't want to warn people off, when really nothing terrible is going to happen.

I will say that the command to remove all the KDE stuff comes from a default Ubuntu installation and takes into account all the packages adding *kubuntu-desktop* would bring with it. The second half of the command also makes sure you still have *ubuntu-desktop* installed so that you're not stuck at a command prompt in case something goes wrong.

I took a quick look at *man apt-get* and didn't see anything like this, but does anyone know if there's an option that is the opposite of *--force-yes*? In other words, an option that would force *dpkg* to ask for confirmation of changes before you go through with the removal?

---

### Post by aysiu on 2008-08-16
I just tried it out myself and was prompted as to whether I wanted the packages removed or not: ```
The following packages will be REMOVED:
  audacity ffmpeg gstreamer0.10-plugins-bad gstreamer0.10-plugins-ugly icewm
  imlib11 kdelibs-data kdelibs4c2a kolourpaint krename libarts1c2a libartsc0
  libaudio2 libavahi-qt3-1 libid3tag0 libimlib2 libjpeg-progs liblua50
  liblualib50 libmodplug0c2 libmpcdec3 libopenexr2c2a libpulse0 libqt3-mt
  libungif4g libxvmc1 mozilla-mplayer mplayer mplayer-fonts
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 29 to remove and 0 not upgraded.
Need to get 0B of archives.
After unpacking 110MB disk space will be freed.
Do you want to continue [Y/n]? n
Abort.
``` I was able to say I did not want to continue after seeing what packages would be removed. I don't really see a problem.

Are others not being prompted for a confirmation?

---

### Post by boddhisatva on 2008-08-17
OK, maybe I overreacted a bit :)
I just didn't see why packages such as Blender should be removed, which are totally desktop-environment independent (when I first installed it I didn't have any KDE components, I don't think you even need to install it to run it). I know I can reinstall all the packages (I already have, most of them), but the first impression was quite distressing, even the screen resolution got screwed up after a re-login, so I feared some serious changes had happened. Now things are back to normal, more or less. Perhaps just a warning saying: 'A lot of your applications (even non-KDE) may be removed in the process' would be useful?
I know it's not such a big deal in the end, but a not very experienced user can freak out for a while when he suddenly can't start the most used applications and the program list is decimated.

On a positive note, keep up the good work :)

P.S. I think the prompt for confirmation was there, but the list of items was huge and I just assumed they were all the KDE components and trusted the uninstallation process. I know, silly me :)

### Post by koshari on 2008-08-21
just a quick tip regarding removing KDE and returning to pure gnome.

if one installed using synaptic the log from the date that kubuntu-desktop package will contain the names of all packages that were added.

so if the user say had amarok for arguments sake installed, then installed kubuntu-dektop , and then wanted to return to remove the majority of KDE components BUT retain amarok, they could use the log file to build a list of packages for apt to remove.

### Post by boddhisatva on 2008-09-08
Sorry to bring up the subject of "Pure Gnome" again, but whereas the recommended method to install KDE via "sudo aptitude update && sudo aptitude install kubuntu-desktop" easily installs kubuntu desktop with all its default applications, the corresponding command "sudo aptitude remove kubuntu-desktop" removes the KDE desktop only but it leaves all the KDE applications behind (visible in the cluttered Gnome menu). So in that sense it doesn't get you back to "pure Gnome". You have to remove all the unneeded applications manually.

---

the pure  xfce command for ubuntu has lots of extra spaces.  I assume the kubuntu part suffers from the same problem.  They are easy to find, they all follow a dash.  So all "- "  should be replaced with "-"

ps - why dont you use the tasksel command for install xubuntu: sudo apt-get install xubuntu-desktop^     ?

### Post by aysiu on 2008-09-19
Thanks for pointing that out. It should be fixed now.

Is there an advantage to using *tasksel*?

---

### Post by danbh on 2008-09-19
Well, I don't know if it has advantage.  Its the command that I used to use to switch between kubuntu or ubuntu or xubuntu.  As you know, just removing ubuntu-desktop and installing xubuntu-desktop, because that just relies on most of the metapackages of the previous installation.  You know this.  The tasksel command seems to go through each package, and install it one by one, that way, getting every package of the installation, and overriding the previous installation.  

Personally, I would use gtkorphan to clean up afterwords.  But, I tested my method, and then yours, and your method clearly got more packages.  I really don't know.  

Besides this question, I have a request.  On your page for creating a separate /home, you use the cpio | find whatever command.  My understanding is that stems  from cp not being very functional, and not supporting sparse files, whatever.  Today, supposedly,  cp can do the copy now, but its still not that great.  

The command I would like to see added as an option is rsync -aS.  Very simple, use it like rsync -aS  /home/. /new_home/.   and it works like a champ (I hope I got the syntax right).  Copies all the files, and maintains permissions and ownerships and I think timestamps even.  

The benefit is that the syntax of the command is clearly, easier to see what is going on, and that you are showing a modern backup command being used in action (which is good for its teaching purposes).

---

### Post by germanix on 2008-10-04
I used your tutorial to create a new home partition in Ubuntu. It did not work for me and in the end I had to new install. I am not blaming your tutorial. I encountered three problems which complicated the matter for me.
The first and second problem were identical and concerns the 2 commands below:

cd /old/home
find . -depth -print0 | sudo cpio --null --sparse -pvd /new/
sudo mv /old/home /old/home_backup
sudo mkdir /old/home

/dev/hda7 /home ext3 nodev,nosuid 0 2

In the first command I was not sure if third word in the command namely -print0
ended with a capitol letter "O" or a zero "0", and in the case of the second command was this a "O" or a "0" in nosuid 0 2,
Looking at it now in this thread it is obvious that it is a zero, but on your tutorial you used a black background, that made it impossible to spot the dot inside the circle. I assumed it was an capitol O.and entered it as such.

The third problem was when I got to the nano text, your tutorial says add this line: /dev/hda7 /home ext3 nodev,nosuid 0 2 
Well as a newbie to Linux and the command line etc, I had never seen the nano texter before, and the cursor was blinking right at the top line, and here I then gave the command.I do not know if this was correct or if I should have moved the cursor down (if that was at all possible)
Anyway if I did add it at the wrong line, I also added it wrong as I still believed the 0 was an O.
(I know I could have done a copy/paste of the commands, but I was reading the tutorial on my laptop whilst working on my PC, and anyway I wanted to gain some experience with typing the commands myself))
The pc crashed and would not boot again, not even with the Ubuntu live cd. I had to go in with a live GParted, delete all partitions and re-install.
Before trying this again I would like you to confirm that it is a zero, and where/which line in the nano texter the last command should go in.
May I further suggest that you change the background of the commands from the current black in your tutorial to some background that will show the dot inside the circle of the zero, to prevent others from making the same mistake.
Apart from these few issues it is a good tutorial, and if it works, which unfortunately I cannot yet comment on, I will be very pleased.
Have a nice day.

### Post by mashcaster on 2008-11-11
Can I make a suggestion of a light weight bare bone system which has a usable modern desktop and makes use of prism for people who want word/spreadsheet applications?

after installing a base ubuntu 8.04 command line system, type in the following.

> sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/lxde.list << EOF
deb [http://ppa.launchpad.net/lxde/ubuntu](http://ppa.launchpad.net/lxde/ubuntu) hardy main
deb-src [http://ppa.launchpad.net/lxde/ubuntu](http://ppa.launchpad.net/lxde/ubuntu) hardy main
EOF

sudo apt-get update && sudo aptitude install xserver-xorg xterm slim lxde obconf lxtask lxrandr kazehakase flashplugin-nonfree prism

Choose "slim" when given the choice of login managers

Note: Sets up a light weight 8.04 system using about 1Gb HDD, and 50Mb ram once the desktop has loaded.  Not tested on 8.10 yet.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2008-11-20
> **caljohnsmith said:**
> Hi Aysiu, I think there might be a really small error in your tutorial about moving /home to a different partition, please see this thread:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=986798](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=986798)
I assure you it's not an error. This is how I've addressed it in the tutorial: > Note: I have tested the second command myself, and it works, but some have pointed out it might make sense to preface the commands with sudo in case one of the other users has subdirectories manually marked as unreadable to the user making the move. Since I have not tested this out and all directories and readable to all by default, I'm offering this as only an alternative in case the command as given does not work:
sudo find . -depth -print0 | sudo cpio --null --sparse -pvd /new/  In fact, I've found that when people use *sudo* to copy that they end up with all sorts of permission and ownership issues later and sometimes aren't able to log in.

In any case, I don't know why my tutorial gets so much flack, since it's really just a step-by-step new-user-friendly version of another tutorial that doesn't get any flack.

I have used these steps myself and know that they work as written. I also know some people have had problems with the steps and proposed other solutions, and I know other people have tried to follow those steps and run into other problems.

I don't think there is a perfect, works-all-the-time procedure for a separate /home partition. I've made it very clear that the way I have copied the tutorial from the original Wordpress tutorial has worked for me and that's all I know.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2008-11-20
Sorry. I don't mean to snap at you. I know you mean well. You're just trying to help.

I'm just a little frustrated.

I did not create the steps in the tutorial. I do not know what the best way is to approach it, and I also cannot take every single suggestion people throw at me or my tutorials would be a mess (if you don't believe me, take a look at the Ubuntu Wiki).

The best thing to do is to go to the source, and I've added a huge bold disclaimer at the top of the separate /home tutorial to that effect.

This is exactly how the tutorial was created: [list][*]At one point in my Ubuntu journey I decided I wanted to create a separate /home partition.[*]I did a Google search for how to do it.[*]I came across a Wordpress blog entry that had the steps to do it.[*]I followed those steps, and they worked for me.[*]So I thought, "Hey, those steps are good. But it doesn't look very new-user-friendly in presentation. Maybe I'll rewrite that other person's tutorial with a different look."[*]I used the exact same commands as the original tutorial and dressed up the form of it a little bit.[/list] That's it. Someone else wrote it. It worked for me. I took the same steps and dressed them up a bit. That's it. I'm not a copy or cpio expert. I don't know all the intricacies of it. I just know it worked for me, and I wanted to make a friendlier-looking version of it.

Normally, I own the tutorials I write because I have actually written them. In this case, I took someone else's tutorial and added a few screenshots, so I'm going to deflect any criticism of it, since I did not create the steps or the commands, and I have a lot of disclaimers at the top that people should read.

At least I can say for its present form "It worked for me." If I took every suggestion thrown my way and people still encountered problems every now and then, all I'd be able to say is "Someone told me that'd work, so I changed my tutorial."

---

### Post by Kinstonian on 2008-11-22
I was just reading some of the security section of psychocats today and liked the part where you made the point that not running as root won't protect you from everything.

I think the people who say Ubuntu doesn't need a firewall because it doesn't have ports open make a few erroneous assumptions...

1.  **Firewalls are only useful for filtering inbound connections**:  If you only look at a firewall as filtering inbound connections, you only see half of it's potential.  What about filtering *outbound* connections with suspicious protocols, or to known bad IPs like the [Russian Business Network](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_Business_Network)?

2.  **All protocols use ports**:  There are protocols like ICMP which don't use ports.  It's not only commonly used for information gathering in attacks, but it's not unreasonable to think history could repeat itself and there could be a vulnerability in the way it's handled by the OS.

3.  **Firewalls are only for preventing attacks**:  While they are no doubt preventative controls, by analyzing the logs they generate they can detect both failed and successful attacks.  If you look at firewall logs from inbound traffic you can detect attacks and take actions.  If you look at firewall logs from outbound traffic like outbound IRC or SMTP traffic to China was blocked, you could have detected a successful attack.

4.  **At no point will there ever be an open port**:  There is a chance that at some point someone could open a port.  Whether it was from the administrator making a mistake in installing/configuring software, or it could be an attacker purposefully using your computer as a server to store and distribute contraband, or perhaps using your computer to host a phishing site.

I think there two of those points were brought up in the guide, so kudos for that.

---

### Post by Hooya on 2009-01-24
I have an update to give on this tutorial:

[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/separatehome](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/separatehome)

When attempting this with my Intrepid install (I'm preparing to downgrade back to Hardy) I couldn't complete this command:

$find . -depth -print0 | cpio &#8211;null &#8211;sparse -pvd /mnt/newhome/

even when starting it as "sudo" - I always got permission errors.

My solution, which I suggest be added to the tutorial, is to start the command line process by becoming su, which requires creating a password for su while booted into the live CD.  The first command after re-partitioning should therefore be:

$sudo passwd root

then enter a short password like "god"

$su
password: god

Now every command is done as super user by default, and the large copy command will work.  This shortens all of the mounting and mkdir commands as well because "sudo" is assumed.

---

### Post by danbh on 2009-01-24
Hey Hooya,
I think that command is an old way of doing things.  I've written my own tutorial, and linked to the other tutorials that I worked off of.  You can see my work here: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Partitioning/Home](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Partitioning/Home) and [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Partitioning/Home/Moving](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Partitioning/Home/Moving)

Secondly Hooya, there is no need to set the root password.  You can just run: sudo su.  Also, purposely setting the root password to a very weak password seems like a bad idea.

Take care,
Dan

---

### Post by aysiu on 2009-02-05
> **cb474 said:**
> Regarding your page on iTunes:

[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/itunes](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/itunes)

What about running iTunes in a virtualized version of Windows or OS X, with something like VMware or VirtualBox? I realize this is not for the faint of heart. But hypothetically speaking, wouldn't it allow you to have iTunes effectively running within a window in your Ubuntu system? So then you wouldn't have to dual-boot. That might be a solution for someone who really wants to have access to the iTunes download services.
I think I've already mentioned that: > If you must run iTunes, your best bet is a dual boot. This means you have to reboot your computer every time you want to access iTunes and then reboot your computer again if you want to use Ubuntu. Two slightly more convenient variations of this are running virtual Ubuntu inside Windows using VMWare (or **running virtual Windows inside Ubuntu using VMWare**) or having two computers and a KVM switch that allows you to quickly switch back and forth between Windows and Ubuntu using the same monitor and keyboard.

All of those are workarounds.

### Post by JoshuaRL on 2009-03-06
Hello aysiu!  On [this page here,](http://psychocats.net/ubuntu/puregnome) I think it might be easier if you used aptitude to remove the desktops.  It would work no matter what version you were running too.
```
sudo aptitude remove kubuntu-desktop
```
You probably know this WAY better than me, but that would work because aptitude can keep track of metapackages and remove them without trouble.

Just thought I'd pass that along.  Thanks for the great site!

---

### Post by aysiu on 2009-03-06
> **JoshuaRL said:**
> Hello aysiu!  On [this page here,](http://psychocats.net/ubuntu/puregnome) I think it might be easier if you used aptitude to remove the desktops.  It would work no matter what version you were running too.
```
sudo aptitude remove kubuntu-desktop
```
You probably know this WAY better than me, but that would work because aptitude can keep track of metapackages and remove them without trouble.

Just thought I'd pass that along.  Thanks for the great site!
On my old pages, I used to recommend aptitude, but it works only if you used aptitude to install the package in the first place, and aptitude can also be funkily over-aggressive in removing packages and remove too much; so I took it out. Thanks for the suggestion, though.

### Post by OldGrey on 2009-03-08
Hello Aysiu,

I have recently installed the Kubuntu desktop in Ubuntu as per your instructions. One of the points you make is that the applications menus become infested with the programs from the alternate desktop. I have recently come across an article in Softpedia which has an idea to tidy that up. 

[http://news.softpedia.com/news/How-to-Install-KDE-4-2-on-Ubuntu-8-10-106118.shtml](http://news.softpedia.com/news/How-to-Install-KDE-4-2-on-Ubuntu-8-10-106118.shtml)

If you scroll towards the end of the article it recommends a couple of little programs which sorts this out by putting all the KDE or Gnome launchers (depending on which desktop you are using) together in one sub menu. I have tried them, they work well and it makes the applications menu much tidier. 

The article also suggests using Startupmanager to enable you to change the splashscreen back to Ubuntu (is you wish), as installing the KDE desktop overwrites the Ubuntu splashscreen.

Hope this helps.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2009-05-08
I installed with the mini.iso for Jaunty not too long ago. Yes, it does (like the earlier installers) need an internet connection, as there's no way a 10 MB file will contain the necessary packages for even a minimal installation.

And, no, you do not have to install all of Gnome.

### Post by nhasian on 2009-08-25
Hello aysiu,

first i want to thank you for your wonderful ubuntu guides.  they are very helpful for newcomers and i frequently point people there for help.

I see you have a guide for installing ubuntu only, another for using wubi, and one for installing on a virtual machine.

didnt you use to have a guide for dual booting with shrinking the windows partition and installing onto a separate partition?  thats probably the most common way people install ubuntu.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2009-08-25
> **nhasian said:**
> Hello aysiu,

first i want to thank you for your wonderful ubuntu guides.  they are very helpful for newcomers and i frequently point people there for help.

I see you have a guide for installing ubuntu only, another for using wubi, and one for installing on a virtual machine.

didnt you use to have a guide for dual booting with shrinking the windows partition and installing onto a separate partition?  thats probably the most common way people install ubuntu. Yes, I had that one before Wubi got really polished.

Now I advise any dual-booters to use Wubi. It's the safest way for a new user to set up a dual-boot. No worries about repartitioning or losing data. No Windows boot loader getting replaced with Grub. If she decides she doesn't like Ubuntu, she can uninstall it as she would any normal Windows program.

I think these are the ways I would go (in this order) for trying out Ubuntu coming from Windows:

1. Try live CD

2. Virtualize inside Windows

3. Dual-boot with Wubi

If she then decides she likes it enough to get rid of Windows entirely, she can just install Ubuntu right over Windows. I think a traditional (non-Wubi) dual-boot should be less common than it is. But there are plenty of guides for that out there if people really want to do one.

### Post by Squash101 on 2009-09-13
I just finished using the "Create a separate home partition in Ubuntu" tutorial using 9.04. This was an excellent guide and I learned a lot, it is most appreciated!

I had a little trouble with the ownership rights when transferring from my old home to the new one. I was getting errors such as "permission denied" even when I was using "sudo" before the command.

The only way I could resolve this was to manually create the user directory in the new home and change the ownership

mkdir /new/*username*
chown -R *username*:*username* /new/*username*

This was done BEFORE running the find command.

All else worked. Thanks!

### Post by earthpigg on 2009-09-15
> **Dangger said:**
> 
I was wondering if you are planning on adding a tutorial on how to install the LXDE desktop in ubuntu as well as a getting back to a pure Gnome after installing LXDE. 


hi,

LXDE is pretty modular, which is awesome.

[http://wiki.lxde.org/en/Main_Page](http://wiki.lxde.org/en/Main_Page)

remove the ubuntu LXDE metapackage, then all the 'components' listed there on the main page. or, just look in synaptic for "LX..." and remove them all.

then remove openbox.
then remove pcmanfm.
then remove leafpad.


with LXDE, you can mix/match/add/remove individual components to your hearts desire and even use them with GNOME in a hybrid desktop setup - for example, gnome's menu editor (alacarte) will work with LXDE's menus, and a minor tweak documented [here]("http://sites.google.com/site/masonux/home/notes-to-myself") will make gnome's networking applet work in lxde. ive played with using gnome-menu in LXDE, and i've played with using LXpanel on top of plain old openbox.

(this is possible because gnome and lxde are both freedesktop.org compliant, both use gtk, and the folks at lxde make great decisions.)

### Post by sisco311 on 2009-10-12
> **aysiu said:**
> Sorry. At the time I wrote that tutorial, Ubuntu was defaulting to using Vi, which is not user-friendly (even though for many advanced users it is easier to use) in comparison to Nano.

Now it appears to be using Nano again, so I'll probably revise that page for the Karmic Koala release.

visudo honors the VISUAL and EDITOR environment variables:

```
EDITOR=nano visudo
```
should open the file in nano, even if the default editor is vi.

---

```

### Post by sisco311 on 2009-10-12
> **t0p said:**

A tutorial on editing .bashrc would probably be useful to newbies.  You could include instruction on how to set the EDITOR to something more newbie-friendly than vi.  (I'm aware that many Linux users love vi, and I don't want to restart any holy wars here... but IMHO vi sucks!  :p )

Yes, in debian vi is the default editor. The Ubuntu developers usually change it to nano, but not in all releases. 

You can set the default editor with:
```
sudo update-alternatives --config editor
```

Can you confirm, that vi is the default in Jaunty?

```
update-alternatives --query editor
```

I'm just curious. :)

### Post by aysiu on 2009-10-16
> **caue.rego said:**
> Just a thought - couldn't find any date of this post: [http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/firefox](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/firefox)

Shouldn't it be dated so we know how outdated or updated is the info on it?

Thanks.
From [the site's introduction](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/index): > **How often do you update this site? Are there any out-of-date tutorials?**
Ubuntu itself has a new release every six months (once in April, once in October of each year). After a new release, it usually takes me a couple of weeks to get the tutorials on this site updated to account for new features in Ubuntu.

If I don't consider the changes worth significantly modifying my tutorials over, I will still make sure the tutorial isn't obsolete.

The tutorials linked directly off the sidebar navigation of this site should all work on the latest version of Ubuntu, even if the screenshots are slightly old. In most cases, I will even include a disclaimer telling you exactly what is outdated. 

---

### Post by earthpigg on 2009-10-16
> **longtom said:**
> 
While on the topic...

Could I "build" my own Ubuntu mix on a virtual system, make a bootable disk with remastersys and use it to install on a PC of my choice?

Thanks in advance.

[next post]

Would I not be able to write a CD (or DVD) in VirtualBox?  I do that now with XP in VB.

I aso have access to my 'mother' machine over the network.  That would mean I could shift the iso file wherever I would need it.  What am I not thinking of?

I tried man ssh....half of it I struggle to get my old head arround.  How would I use it in this particular case?

Thank you.

I know this is several months to late, but for anyone in the future wishing to do this... it isn't that hard to make VirtualBox share folders with the host system.

no need for ssh or uploading it to the internet somewhere in the guest machine and downloading it from the host machine.

do this ***after*** creating the .iso using remastersys within virtualbox on the guest machine.

the host machine can run any operating system you wish, so long as the ***proprietary*** version of VirtualBox is installed. (X breaks for me on the guest system if i use the OSE version in the repos.)

> ***Step 5: Setup vbox to share folders***

run as root on guest machine:
```
apt-get install make gcc linux-headers-2.6.28-15-generic #replace 2.6.28-15-generic with the kernel you are using. Find it by running uname -a.
```

config vbox shared folder name to be 'share'

install vbox guest additions. devices -> install guest additions.
```
sudo pcmanfm #gotta install from the "cd" as root.
```
run the script on the fake mounted 'cd'


completely power off the VM and restart it.
```
mkdir /media/rawr
mount -t vboxsf share /media/rawr
cp /home/remastersys/remastersys/*iso* /media/rawr/.
```

From [here]("http://sites.google.com/site/masonux/home/notes-to-myself"). It is not intended primarily as an easy-to-understand tutorial, so it isn't nearly as well 'put together' as anything done by Aysiu.

If anyone wants to steal it and make it easy to understand, you are welcome to. consider it released under the [WTFPL]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WTFPL").

pcmanfm comes with LXDE, so if your system uses nautilus or something else, replace the 'pcmanfm' command above with 'nautilus' or whatever. or just do it directly from the command line.

### Post by sisco311 on 2009-11-04
> **aysiu said:**
> Gah! Okay. I'll figure out how to translate it.

Use the *-o udev* option (vol_id compatible mode).  

```
sudo blkid -o udev /dev/sda3
ID_FS_LABEL=home
ID_FS_LABEL_ENC=home
ID_FS_UUID=e2bdf561-94b1-45f0-af97-02d43a1890c1
ID_FS_UUID_ENC=e2bdf561-94b1-45f0-af97-02d43a1890c1
ID_FS_TYPE=ext4

```

---

### Post by kendoori on 2009-11-15
I used the tutorial for moving Home to a new partition and got completely stuck on the ICEauthority error that others have reported. I tried the various remedies to no avail, so I went to backout, and must have mistyped, as the restoration of home went to the wrong folder... 

Since the home_backup folder was intact, I decided to use rsync to copy my the home_backup folder to the new partition I'd created on sda4. I believe the method described in the tutorial doesn't always handle permissions properly, and felt that rsync would bring everything across properly. It turns out that this did work properly, so in case anyone else gets the "Count not update ICEauthority file" error when trying to do this, I would suggest trying to recopy your home from a LiveCD using rsync.

Here's the commands I used when running from a LiveCD
```

sudo rsync -av --progress /media/Ubuntu-Main/home_backup/kendoori /media/Home

```

/media/Ubuntu-Main/home_backup/kendoori = location of home files
/media/Home = mount point of my new partition

### Post by sudoer541 on 2009-12-09
[U][I]**> **aysiu said:**
> **[B]For New Users:
[The Psychocats site]("http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/") is a great site for new users to explore. It uses a combination of terminal commands and screenshot-heavy point-and-click tutorials to help get you up and running with Ubuntu, and it addresses many common questions and problems newcomers have when starting with Ubuntu.

Psychocats isn't intended to compete with other documentation sites. Its goal is to give a uniform look and feel for several key tutorials and, more importantly, not to overwhelm or confuse new users with too many tutorials in hard-to-find places. Each tutorial addresses a common Ubuntu question, and all the internal links are on the sidebar.

If you're interested in a more comprehensive set of tutorials, especially ones aimed at advanced or intermediate use (or just anything not covered on my site--for example, how to use ndiswrapper), you may want to check out [the Tutorials & Tips section of these forums,]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=100")[the official community-maintained documentation]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community").

[/B] or [B]For Other Users:
Thank you for all your help so far!




Users on these forums have caught typos and other errors and kept me abreast of changes in versions of software I don't use (e.g. Songbird). Some users have even contributed whole paragraphs to the Psychocats tutorials, and I've tried to credit them appropriately.

[/B][B]Working with Psychocats:
I've had numerous requests asking if people can link to or translate the Psychocats Ubuntu website. The answer is yes. You can link to Psychocats without asking my permission. If you want to translate Psychocats Ubuntu, you may do that as well (please let me know, though--I'm just curious to know what's going on), and I encourage spreading the knowledge. I haven't officially licensed the documentation, but the closest I've found to what I'd say embodies the spirit with which I'm giving Psychocats Ubuntu to the community is [the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 license ]("http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/3.0/"). If you would like to mirror Psychocats, you may do so, but please send me a link to the mirrored site, so I can know to refer people there also.

[/B][B]Future Updates and Feedback:
If you have any constructive criticism, please leave it in this thread. I'll be checking back from time to time. I devote a lot of time to the Ubuntu community and to these forums, especially, but this is not my main job. So go ahead and leave any suggestions you may have, but I may not get around to implementing them right away (or at all, depending on how good the suggestion is).

If the Psychocats website has helped you, you can post that here, too. It's always good to know what people have found helpful, and which pages are most frequently used by people.



[/B][/I][/U]

As a nubuntie, I use this excellent site a lot :)

It seems to me that the only method is to google:

The only problem is that the left menu is so comprehensive that new users might assume that the entire tutorial contents are in that menu, and therefore might assume that there is no flash tutorial :)

So after all the whinging, thanks for the great site :)

### Post by slakkie on 2009-12-18
[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/separatehome](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/separatehome)

Regarding that post.

You recommend not to use a seperate slice for /home if the disk is smaller then 30Gb. I think having a seperate home partition is a valid option even with a 10Gb disk.

Ubuntu uses roughly (depending on your installation) 5-8GB. Excluding logs in /var/log (roughly 500MB). Having a seperate home will make sure that the rest of your disk, 22Gb can be allocated to /home (or less, if you need swap space).

What I would advise is to have a root partition around 5-10Gb, 2Gb swap, and the rest for /home.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2009-12-18

Ubuntu uses roughly (depending on your installation) 5-8GB. Excluding logs in /var/log (roughly 500MB). Having a seperate home will make sure that the rest of your disk, 22Gb can be allocated to /home (or less, if you need swap space).

What I would advise is to have a root partition around 5-10Gb, 2Gb swap, and the rest for /home.
It's just a personal recommendation. Obviously it's up to each user to assess what works best for her.

I appreciate that other people have other preferences, but to a new user who doesn't know any better, I think it's best to stay conservative.

### Post by aysiu on 2009-12-18

What I've found over the years is that you can't make everyone happy, and the wider your audience, the more suggestions are at odds with each other. If I'd recommended people create a separate /home with a 20 GB hard drive, I'd get people suggesting that 20 GB is too small and people will run out of room. If I left out a suggestion altogether, people would suggest I put in a recommendation, since new users won't know what's a reasonable size to have a separate /home partition.

I definitely value each suggestion I get, even suggestions I do not end up implementing. It's good to know the diversity of needs and preferences out there, and my hope is that if my site can't serve particular groups of users then other sites can fill in those gaps.

### Post by phillw on 2009-12-19
Hi,

Firstly, thanks for a gr8 'hands on' I used the methodology.

I hope you don't think that I'm being picky, but .....

With dual-boot systems spare primary partitions are getting rarer than hens teeth & chicken lips (Try going into your local 'sub' and asking for a "Chicken Lip Sandwich, with Mayo, Salad, Hold the onion"...)

I shrunk the partitions I wanted to create unallocated space, then I tried to make the partition on my extended area that had swap on it. 

I'm not sure why, hence the question, but it would not allow me to create a partition within my extended area for the new /home. I had to remove my /swap and then re-add both /swap and /home.

Also, on my trawls on such matters I was advised that rsync is the better method of copying over the old home to the new home area.

```
sudo rsync -aS /old/home. /new/. 
```

Where old & new are as per your tutorial.

I'd be grateful of any response to the above points.

Regards,

Phill.

### Post by phillw on 2009-12-26
> **da burger said:**
> Hi I was reading on the site and it was very interesting. As a recent convert to ubuntu myself it would be nice if you could add something about setting up a native dual boot (particularly from vista). I recently did this and I found it surprisingly easy but it took me a long time (and several questions in the beginners section of this forum) before I had enough knowledge and confidence to try.
Angus

Hi, the author has done a real good job of the tutorial. putting a new /home onto a dual boot system is really fraught with problems. One of them being that some Win installations are using primary partitions as backup areas as they're either..
1) unable to use an extended partition.
2) too lazy to do it.

When I went from 'simple' dual boot to seperate /home and wanted to write a "How To" it quickly became apparent that tagging it onto /dev/sda4 is not an option, so I chain loaded mine into my extended partition. To do this, I had to remove my /swap area and then re-install it - which is no big deal. But a step that shouldn't have to be done.

I went from the standard dual boot of

```

/dev/sda1   *           1        4464    35857048+   7  HPFS/NTFS
/dev/sda2            9601        9729     1036192+   f  W95 Ext'd (LBA)
/dev/sda3            4466        9600    41246887+  83  Linux
/dev/sda5            9602        9729     1028160   82  Linux swap / Solaris

```to something a little more complicated ...

```

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/sda1   *           1        4464    35857048+   7  HPFS/NTFS
/dev/sda2            6654        9729    24707970    5  Extended
/dev/sda3            4466        6653    17575110   83  Linux
/dev/sda5            6654        8037    11116948+  83  Linux
/dev/sda6            8038        9587    12450343+  83  Linux
/dev/sda7            9588        9729     1140583+  82  Linux swap / Solaris

```I've got another computer to do a dual boot of, so, I'm hoping to tidy up my "how To" - whether it is ever good enough for the Ubuntu help pages, i don't know. I tend to write my stuff as I, we & us. As the part of doing the additional extended area and using it, if you'd have a glance over at a posting I'll add a bit later (vpolink is doing it's upgrade from vBulletin 3.x to 4.0x) Maybe you could help out with it as you've already, like me, done it. Just remember that we're looking at only having 1 free primary partition - so, it's going to be a bit 'messy' and we have to keep backups of everything !!!

Phill.

---

### Post by nanotube on 2010-01-01
Hi aysiu

i have modified the ubuntuzilla script to do this stuff automatically (the source code is in the git repo - check the 'technical details' section of the ubuntuzilla website). so the whole "detect latest version, download tar.gz, check gpg sig, extract, package into a .deb, stick it into a repository, upload to sourceforge" procedure is a one-step operation.

at this point, i'm launching the operation manually, but eventually think i'll stick it into a cron job on my webhost's server, and have it run automatically every X hours, so i don't even have to think about this. only way it can ever get borked is if mozilla changes something on its website or release servers to throw the script into confusion ...

heres a correction for you :D
[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/whichbuntu]("http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/whichbuntu")
> 
What's the difference between the Ubuntu CD and DVD?
I'm not 100% certain, but the last I'd heard, the CD usually contains only one version of Ubuntu (Ubuntu, Kubuntu, Xubuntu, Edubuntu) and is either the Desktop CD, the Alternate CD, or the Server CD. The DVD contains the entire Main and Restricted repositories (read more about Main and Restricted here), which means it has Ubuntu, Kubuntu, Xubuntu, and Edubuntu, allowing you to install any or all four versions without needing an internet connection. 



[SIZE="4"]what the DVDs are[/SIZE]
from this
[http://www.ubuntu.com/getubuntu/downloadmirrors#dvd]("http://www.ubuntu.com/getubuntu/downloadmirrors#dvd")
> DVD downloads

Don't be confused, even though DVDs can hold far more data than the typical Ubuntu CD, the main benefit of the DVD downloads is to get access to all of the available language packs. Most people will be fine with the standard CD installer.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-01-12

### Post by nanotube on 2010-01-12
> **aysiu said:**
> We covered this already. That says the language packs are the *main benefit*, not the only difference.

Until someone can verify that the language packs are the only difference between the DVD and the CD, I'm not "correcting" anything.

since i posted to this thread, now i'm kind of following it, so came across this discussion.

here's what i have for you:

here's the full listing of the files contained on the dvd for karmic:
[http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/releases/9.10/release/ubuntu-9.10-dvd-i386.list](http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/releases/9.10/release/ubuntu-9.10-dvd-i386.list)

(go up a few levels, and you should be able to browse over to the same list for older ubuntu releases).

i haven't looked at in in that great of a detail... but:

metapackages ubuntu-desktop and edubuntu-desktop are present, so it is correct so say that edubuntu and ubuntu are present. (which is no big surprise, since edubuntu is just a few edu apps on top of regular ubuntu)

kubuntu-desktop and xubuntu-desktop, however, are NOT present, so it is not correct to say that they're included on the dvd. 

note also that, afaik, 'server' is a subset of 'desktop', so the fact that it includes 'server and desktop' is no big feat. :)

by looking at the various .list files for the cd images, (e.g., here's the list of available 9.10 cds: [http://releases.ubuntu.com/9.10/](http://releases.ubuntu.com/9.10/) ), and running diffs on them, you can figure out /precisely/ what the differences between the various 'types' of cd/dvd are.

p.s. though for some reason the 'desktop' cd .lists are abridged (e.g., [http://releases.ubuntu.com/9.10/ubuntu-9.10-desktop-i386.list](http://releases.ubuntu.com/9.10/ubuntu-9.10-desktop-i386.list)), at least the alternate and server are full listings...  if you really want the desktop .lists gotta dl the iso, mount it, and run a recursive list yourself.


EDIT: also note that the dvd list appears to contain only a handful of the restricted packages: some ati and nvidia drivers, and the broadcom wifi drivers, and that's it.

---

### Post by sisco311 on 2010-01-12
Xubuntu is officially supported, but it's maintained by the community so the Xubuntu related packages are in the universe repo not in the main or restricted.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2010-01-12
nanotube, thanks for posting up some real information and not just speculation. I'll update that bit as soon as I can.

---

### Post by nanotube on 2010-01-12
> **aysiu said:**
> nanotube, thanks for posting up some real information and not just speculation. I'll update that bit as soon as I can.

no hurry... :)

EDIT: so i was curious about the abridged .desktop .lists, so i pulled out a jaunty livecd and took a look.

indeed, the number of files on the disk is small - and the reason is that because it's a livecd, most files are one large 'live filesystem' file, rather than in a repository pool. 

there's a text file on the disk, called 'filesystem.manifest', which contains all the packages that are included on the livecd filesystem. attaching it here for your reference... (zipped because of the super-low filesize limit....)

---

### Post by AMDphreak on 2010-02-21
OK. this for the person who wrote the tutorial.

There is one side-effect of using the fstab method which you have not addressed.
I have used this method myself even before reading your tutorial, so I was looking for an answer to my problem on there, but I did not find it.

My problem:
after mounting with fstab and rebooting or doing "mount -a" I have a double-entry in many of the applications that allow for mounting detected drives.  I mounted my drive, which is called "Candide", to the usual folder that is decided upon when you don't use fstab.

/media/Candide

So after fstabing it to that directory mount-point, I reboot, and it mounts properly into the folder, however, there is then that old entry (or double-entry). It identifies the fstab'd one as a hard drive (i can tell by the icon in the Gnome panel Places menu and the icon in Nautilus's Places menu), but it re-identifies /dev/sda7 as yet another mountable medium, then when I, out of curiosity, click it to see what it does, it attempts to mount /dev/sda7 AGAIN.

mount error message
---
mount: /dev/sda7 already mounted or /media/Candide busy
mount: according to mtab, /dev/sda7 is already mounted on /media/Candide

I tried using a different approach to suppress the dual identifications. My approach was to mount in a folder inside the /mnt directory:
UUID=[some numbers and fun] /mnt/Candide auto rw,auto,user,exec,async 0 2

well---it suppressed the dual identities, but it also hid the mounted volume from the Places menu.

So my question: how do I use the usual mount point /media/something without upsetting the automatic detection of "new" media.

thanks, Ryan

Here is a screenshot of what I'm talking about.
[IMG]http://img39.imageshack.us/img39/6928/fstabglitch.png[/IMG]

### Post by Ian dewhurst on 2010-03-17
The tutorials are really good.
One thing though when I edited fstab it could no longer find /home even when I ran chown and chmod commands in recovery console and it couldn't mount a root filesystemas well.
In fstab I replaced UUID with /dev/sda1 that fixed one problem and creating a new home folder (easy enough) fixed the ICEauthority and .dmrc issues.
I also ended up with duplicate folders like some others.
After that however its worked brilliantly.

### Post by soggydoggy on 2010-04-26

### Post by arnab_das on 2010-04-26
Psychocats to be honest is THE ubuntu help site. when i started using ubuntu psychocats was an absolute necessity. ubuntuforums is great but sometimes detailed outlined user friendly tutorials are a necessity. i think thats where psychocats excels. i dont think i would be using ubuntu today had it not been for psychocats. i guess thats the biggest compliment i can give.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-04-26
Thank you arnab_das. I'm glad you have found my tutorials to be useful in your Ubuntu journey.

I don't really see Psychocats as in competition with other kinds of help. It is a handful of carefully crafted screenshot-laden tutorials focused on ex-Windows users who are totally new to the world of Linux in general and Ubuntu specifically.

It is not comprehensive. And it is not for those who really want to get their hands dirty or deal with a lot of technical terms. The Ubuntu Manual is exciting, because it appears to be very comprehensive and professional-looking. The community site is great, because it often does go into a lot of technical details. The Ubuntu Guide has one-stop shopping for quick copy-and-paste commands.

The Ubuntu Forums aren't as much about tutorials as they are about troubleshooting problems or just connecting with the rest of the Ubuntu community.

---

### Post by philobyte on 2010-08-07
[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/ubuntuzilla](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/ubuntuzilla)

Love the tutorials in general, but found this one confused someone totally.  The problem is that there are two things going on at once, the the tutorial doesn't spell it out well enough:

-- download the key.txt file for signing.
-- download the apt sources.list entry.

the order in the tutorial is:
-- get the key file.
-- get the list entry.
-- apply the list entry
-- apply the key file.

The user naturally assumed that they are going to use the thing they just created, and skipped the whole get the list entry part.

It's kind of a nested method of working, do something, save it, 
do something else, then apply the thing you saved before.

Also, when you add the list entry, it rebuilds, and you get an error because the key file isn't there yet.

Suggest this be re-ordered:

Section:  Add UbuntuZilla Repository Signing Key
-- get the key file.
-- apply the key file.

Section: Add Ubuntuzilla Repository
-- get the list entry.
-- apply the list entry.

That way the user is more aware that they are doing two things, and do not have to manage two separate items at once.
Then when applying the list entry, reload will be triggerred automatically, and succeed.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2010-08-08
> **philobyte said:**
> [http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/ubuntuzilla](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/ubuntuzilla)

Love the tutorials in general, but found this one confused someone totally.  The problem is that there are two things going on at once, the the tutorial doesn't spell it out well enough:

-- download the key.txt file for signing.
-- download the apt sources.list entry.

the order in the tutorial is:
-- get the key file.
-- get the list entry.
-- apply the list entry
-- apply the key file.

The user naturally assumed that they are going to use the thing they just created, and skipped the whole get the list entry part.

It's kind of a nested method of working, do something, save it, 
do something else, then apply the thing you saved before.

Also, when you add the list entry, it rebuilds, and you get an error because the key file isn't there yet.

Suggest this be re-ordered:

Section:  Add UbuntuZilla Repository Signing Key
-- get the key file.
-- apply the key file.

Section: Add Ubuntuzilla Repository
-- get the list entry.
-- apply the list entry.

That way the user is more aware that they are doing two things, and do not have to manage two separate items at once.
Then when applying the list entry, reload will be triggerred automatically, and succeed.
Thanks for the suggestion. I'll put that on my to-do list.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-08-08
> **aysiu said:**
> Thanks for the suggestion. I'll put that on my to-do list.
Okay, I've updated it. Unfortunately, it is less confusing in one way but more confusing in another.

On the other hand, now that I've updated it, it's not confusing, but it is a bit awkward to go to the web browser, go to Software Sources, switch back to the web browser, and then switch back to Software Sources again.

If I get any complaints, I'll switch it back to the original.

### Post by mörgæs on 2010-08-11
Regarding the tutorial on the minimal Ubuntu:

As of now, the text focuses on how to apply IceWM. If you have the time, it would be nice with a few commands for setting up the Gnome window manager and other relevant packages, so the look and feel will be closer to the regular Ubuntu.

I know that this will make the system a little heavier, but still much lighter than the full monty.

Apart from this the tutorial is great.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-08-11
> **mörgæs said:**
> Regarding the tutorial on the minimal Ubuntu:

As of now, the text focuses on how to apply IceWM. If you have the time, it would be nice with a few commands for setting up the Gnome window manager and other relevant packages, so the look and feel will be closer to the regular Ubuntu.

I know that this will make the system a little heavier, but still much lighter than the full monty.

Apart from this the tutorial is great.
I don't think that warrants inclusion in the tutorial. The point of the tutorial is to be minimal, not to cover all possible custom-install situations.

If you personally would like to know how to bring in Gnome elements, just install the *gnome-core* package instead of *ubuntu-desktop*.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-09-13
If you want to get back to pure Ubuntu Gnome from Lubuntu, try this: ```
sudo apt-get remove abiword abiword-common abiword-plugin-grammar abiword-plugin-mathview aqualung arj cheese cheese-common chromium-browser chromium-browser-inspector chromium-codecs-ffmpeg elementary-icon-theme epdfview galculator giblib1 gnome-mplayer gnumeric gnumeric-common gnumeric-doc gpicview hardinfo keyutils leafpad libabiword-2.8 libaiksaurus-1.2-0c2a libaiksaurus-1.2-data libaiksaurusgtk-1.2-0c2a libaudio2 libavcodec52 libavformat52 libavutil49 libburn4 libcddb2 libcheese-gtk18 libcompfaceg1 libdiscid0 libdvdnav4 libdvdread4 libenca0 libexo-0.3-0 libexo-common libgdome2-0 libgdome2-cpp-smart0c2a libgoffice-0.8-8 libgoffice-0.8-8-common libgringotts2 libgsm1 libgtkmathview0c2a libid3tag0 libifp4 libimlib2 libisofs6 libjpeg-progs liblink-grammar4 liblrdf0 liblua5.1-0 liblzo2-2 libmad0 libmcrypt4 libmenu-cache1 libmhash2 libmodplug0c2 libmp3lame0 libmpcdec3 libmusicbrainz3-6 libobparser21 libobrender21 liboggz2 libonig2 libopenal1 libots0 libpostproc51 libpsiconv6 librpm0 librpmbuild0 librpmio0 libschroedinger-1.0-0 libsexy2 libsvga1 libswscale0 libt1-5 libthunar-vfs-1-2 libuniconf4.6 libvpx0 libwv-1.2-3 libwvstreams4.6-base libwvstreams4.6-extras libx264-85 libxfce4util-bin libxfce4util-common libxfce4util4 libxfcegui4-4 libxfconf-0-2 libxvidcore4 link-grammar-dictionaries-en lubuntu-artwork lubuntu-default-settings lubuntu-desktop lubuntu-plymouth-theme lxappearance lxde-common lxde-core lxdm lxinput lxlauncher lxmenu-data lxpanel lxrandr lxsession lxsession-edit lxshortcut lxterminal mplayer mtpaint ntp obconf openbox openbox-themes osmo p7zip-full parcellite pcmanfm pidgin pidgin-data pidgin-libnotify powernowd pyneighborhood rpm rpm-common rpm2cpio scrot smbfs sylpheed sylpheed-i18n tcl thunar-data transmission transmission-cli ttf-lyx wvdial xarchiver xchat xchat-common xfburn xfce-keyboard-shortcuts xfce4-taskmanager xfconf xscreensaver xserver-xorg-input-evtouch && sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop
``` As I said before, I doubt it'll be a regular part of Psychocats tutorials, but you may find it helpful in this one instance (works only for Lucid).

### Post by phillw on 2010-10-14
Hiyas Boss,

for the installation of lubuntu, I'm told that the command should be ```
sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends lubuntu-desktop
``` as it stops dragging in some extra stuff. There's more on it at [Installing Lubuntu](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Lubuntu/DocumentationHelp#Install Lubuntu from Ubuntu or any Ubuntu flavors)

As ever, Thanks,

Phill.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2010-10-14
> **phillw said:**
> Hiyas Boss,

for the installation of lubuntu, I'm told that the command should be ```
sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends lubuntu-desktop
``` as it stops dragging in some extra stuff. There's more on it at [Installing Lubuntu](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Lubuntu/DocumentationHelp#Install Lubuntu from Ubuntu or any Ubuntu flavors)

As ever, Thanks,

Phill.
That's not specific to Lubuntu.

Recent versions of Ubuntu install "recommended" packages along with any package (or metapackage) you install.

So if you don't want extra stuff, you should use *--no-install-recommends* for whatever you're installing, not just Lubuntu.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2010-10-14

I've gone through each of the main tutorials and made sure they work for Maverick. In some cases, where appropriate, I've updated screenshots.

> **jimr17 said:**

Shoot. I thought I'd double-checked that, but yeah apparently it's not in Software Center for Maverick.

Go to System > Administration > Synaptic Package Manager and install *kubuntu-desktop*

### Post by mezentius on 2011-04-17
On the "Mounting Windows Partitions in Ubuntu" tutorial ([http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/mountwindows]("http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/mountwindows")) it tells you to install ntfs-config. There's currently a bug that prevents ntfs-config from starting up normally. The solution (from [here]("http://www.ubunturoot.com/2010/11/fix-ntfs-configuration-tool-in-ubuntu.html")) is simply to run

[FONT="Courier New"]sudo mkdir -p /etc/hal/fdi/policy[/FONT]

and then proceed as normal. Anyway, thanks for all your help!

### Post by Dangertux on 2011-10-18
Really nice job with these :-)

EDIT: Although there is something you might wish to address in regards to this : [http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/security](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/security)

The below in bold is slightly innacurate.

> 
By default, Ubuntu ships with no open ports on public interfaces. In   other words, a "port scan" would show all closed ports, nothing open. **As   a result, putting up a firewall would provide no more security than  not  putting one up. Remember that open ports provide services that  hackers  can connect to, and only if they can connect to these services  can they  be potentially abused and exploited.**  A firewall, however,  adds the benefit of peace-of-mind from accidentally  installing a  server program that opens up a port by default. Also, it  satisfies  curiosity by logging potential "hits." Linux comes with a very  strong,  secure, and powerful firewall called *iptables*, but it is   relatively difficult to use from a new user's standpoint. As a result,   there are many graphical tools that give you a simple user interface for   configuring *iptables*, such as Firestarter for GNOME or Guarddog for KDE. There are many more in the repository, too. Remember&#8212;these *all*   use iptables in the background, so find your favorite interface&#8212;they   all offer the same great protection. [These last two paragraphs   contributed by *[jdong]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=780")* from the Ubuntu Forums. Thanks, jdong!] 
  

It is 50% accurate in that it accounts for services being bound to ports  and exploited traditionally. Think IAC Telnet buffer overflow in  ProFTPd 1.3.2a 

However consider this situation, you are browsing in Firefox and you  encounter a malicious site that capitalizes on a vulnerability in  Firefox that allows remote code execution.

Now say that the code executed were a reverse connection instead of the  typical "open a port" bind shell. Even in its default state UFW would  have the following rules (in terms of iptables)

```

iptables -P INPUT DROP
iptables -P FORWARD DROP
iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

```
The reverse connection would first match the default OUTPUT  policy of ACCEPT and be allowed to establish the remote connection to  the attacker's machine. Then the returning traffic would match the last  rule as ESTABLISHED,RELATED because it was in fact a solicited  connection. Thus bypassing the default firewall. In the case that no  firewall were present either a bind shell or reverse shell would be  successful.

You should consider updating it to include more restrictive outbound rules to limit what a reverse shell would be allowed to do.

Information on that can be found in this article : 
[http://blog.bodhizazen.net/linux/firewall-ubuntu-servers/](http://blog.bodhizazen.net/linux/firewall-ubuntu-servers/)

Information more in depth with examples of what I discussed about bypassing firewalls can be found here : 
[http://dangertux.wordpress.com/2011/10/03/a-firewall-is-not-a-catch-all/](http://dangertux.wordpress.com/2011/10/03/a-firewall-is-not-a-catch-all/)

Hope this is helpful

Latest version is 0.9.9 and PCMan has sent us an Email the other day saying he got more time now to work on his File Manager (PCManFM). Not sure when that will be though.

It's your call. I'd suggest to use whatever you like and/or happy with :)
I used to run both Ubuntu and Lubuntu but that's history now, I'm all into Lubuntu :)

@ lazylew: you can have both desktops (or more) in the same Ubuntu install. All you have to do is
Code:
---------
sudo apt-get install lubuntu-desktop
---------
from your current installation (XUbuntu or whatever *Ubuntu).
You can choose at boot time which desktop you want for the session.

Of course, you'll have installed all the apps of Xub & Lub, and they're all seen in the menu.

### Post by Zookalicious on 2011-11-14
Fantastic website first of all, I've used it more times than I care to count. One thing I noticed, is that it might be worth updating [http://psychocats.net/ubuntu/classicgnome](http://psychocats.net/ubuntu/classicgnome) to take into account that the Indicator Applets recently got ported to classic gnome: [http://www.webupd8.org/2011/11/indicator-applet-ported-to-gnome-3-can.html](http://www.webupd8.org/2011/11/indicator-applet-ported-to-gnome-3-can.html) which really helps with the appearance and usability of classic mode in 11.10. Also if you change the panel background settings to "Solid Colour" and zero transparency it gets rid of the unpleasant bubble effect that you see around the panel applets. Oh and customization of panels requires holding Alt+right clicking.

Also Compiz can be used to get desktop effects back. There is probably a nicer way to do it, but I have just added "compiz --replace" to my startup items and it seems to do the trick. Final result here: [http://i.imgur.com/JXuF4.jpg](http://i.imgur.com/JXuF4.jpg) and I think it looks pretty good in comparison to Gnome 3.23. 

Just some thoughts in case you were interested. Thanks again for the excellent learning tools!

### Post by nooby on 2011-12-15
Hi aysiu,

thanks for the tutorials. 

As I get it what you recommend works for standard install of Ubuntu. 
Full install on HDD or USB while I have a kind of no install. 
A cheating way of install. 

So I don't have that recovery mode as you show in the youtube video. 

Here is how my code look in grub4dos. 

```
 title lubuntu 11.10 
 find --set-root --ignore-floppies --ignore-cd /lubuntu-10.10.iso
 kernel	/lubuntu/casper/vmlinuz  file=/cdrom/preseed/ubuntu.seed boot=casper iso-scan/filename=/lubuntu-10.10.iso noeject noprompt quiet splash --
 initrd	/lubuntu/casper/initrd.lz


 title xubuntu 11.10 
 find --set-root --ignore-floppies --ignore-cd /xubuntu-11.10.iso
 kernel	/xubuntu/casper/vmlinuz  file=/cdrom/preseed/xubuntu.seed boot=casper iso-scan/filename=/xubuntu-11.10.iso noeject noprompt quiet splash --
 initrd	/xubuntu/casper/initrd.lz
```

 So it is a kind of frugal iso "live session user" boot on NTFS formatted HD. 

It works very well but is a cheat. So I would prefer to be able to boot 
in recovery mode instead of being "live session user". 

Anyway what is needed as seen above is three things. 
the iso placed on the drive you want to boot from and 
a Dir named after the iso example 
lubuntu or xubuntu and you copy from the iso the casper and preseed dirs
into this lubuntu or xubuntu dir and then the title to be added to the grub4dos menu.lst 

It allow me to edit text and html files and save them back. It allows me to delete and add directories and move files to them. 
I can have lubuntu and xubuntu and ubuntu and linux mint on same NTFS HD and boot them. So all is good except it is not as the Ubuntu Devs wants it. 

So the better way would be to do a regular single mode frugal boot. 

Maybe you can help with that? What should I change in this boot code to accomplish that?

### Post by aysiu on 2011-12-23
I don't *suggest* either a dual-boot or a virtual machine. I mention both of them as *workarounds*: > If you must run iTunes, your best bet is a dual boot. This means you have to reboot your computer every time you want to access iTunes and then reboot your computer again if you want to use Ubuntu. Two slightly more convenient variations of this are running virtual Ubuntu inside Windows using Virtualbox (or running virtual Windows inside Ubuntu using Virtualbox) or having two computers and a KVM switch that allows you to quickly switch back and forth between Windows and Ubuntu using the same monitor and keyboard.

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-04-08
I've been working on some non-pae installation/conversion options for 12.04 and wanted to give you a heads up in case you hadn't already considered the implications for the DE conversion part of your great tutorials.

You probably know that Ubuntu, Kubuntu, and probably Edubuntu will ship with the PAE kernel in 12.04, but both Lubuntu and Xubuntu will ship with non-pae.

As I've tried to parse the conversion from a non-pae Lubuntu install to Ubuntu it's important to note that one must be sure to watch what's going on with the packages "linux-headers-<version-number>-generic-pae" and "linux-headers-generic-pae", or most importantly the removal of the non-pae versions if converting Lubuntu or Xubuntu to one of the other DE's on a machine that lacks PAE kernel support.

On a side note, probably something you've already considered, I find it's best to never remove any plymouth theme or lightdm greeter packages.

If you wish you can read more here:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1951653](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1951653)

Hope I'm not just being a pain in your neck ;)

---
