### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-04-28
I finally had time to do a bit more playing with converting a Precise Lubuntu to pure Ubuntu. I'd already mentioned [the pae vs non-pae kernel]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1959675") issue but that was simpler to figure out than 'lightdm' and even slightly 'plymouth'.

Anyway what I did to begin with was install a fresh Lubuntu and update it, then installed 'ubuntu-desktop' and did a few reboots to be sure that both Ubuntu and Lubuntu would still boot and perform reasonably well. 

I should note here that allowing the installation of pae versions of 'linux-headers-<version_number>-generic', and 'linux-headers-generic' is not a problem. At this point things will remain non-pae as long as the pae linux-image packages are NOT installed, no problem ;)

Next I cooked this up and ran it from the Ubuntu desktop using gnome-terminal:

```
sudo apt-get remove abiword abiword-common abiword-plugin-grammar abiword-plugin-mathview ace-of-penguins audacious audacious-plugins audacious-plugins-data blueman chromium-browser chromium-browser-l10n chromium-codecs-ffmpeg elementary-icon-theme esound-common galculator gecko-mediaplayer giblib1 gnome-desktop-data gnome-icon-theme-full gnome-mplayer gnome-system-tools gnome-time-admin gnumeric gnumeric-common gnumeric-doc gpicview guvcview hardinfo indicator-status-provider-pidgin leafpad libaacs0 libabiword-2.9 libaudclient2 libaudcore1 libaudiofile1 libbinio1ldbl libbluray1 libbs2b0 libcddb2 libcolamd2.7.1 libcompfaceg1 libcue1 libencode-locale-perl libesd0 libexo-1-0 libexo-common libexo-helpers libfile-listing-perl libfluidsynth1 libfm-data libfm-gtk-data libfm-gtk1 libfm1 libfont-afm-perl libgdome2-0 libgdome2-cpp-smart0c2a libgif4 libglade2-0 libgmlib0 libgmtk0 libgmtk0-data libgoffice-0.8-8 libgoffice-0.8-8-common libgringotts2 libgsf-1-114 libgsf-1-common libgtkmathview0c2a libgtkspell0 libguess1 libhtml-form-perl libhtml-format-perl libhtml-parser-perl libhtml-tagset-perl libhtml-tree-perl libhttp-cookies-perl libhttp-daemon-perl libhttp-date-perl libhttp-message-perl libhttp-negotiate-perl libid3tag0 libimlib2 libio-socket-inet6-perl libio-socket-ssl-perl libjavascriptcoregtk-1.0-0 libjpeg-progs libjpeg-turbo-progs liblaunchpad-integration1 liblink-grammar4 libloudmouth1-0 liblwp-mediatypes-perl liblwp-protocol-https-perl libmailtools-perl libmcrypt4 libmenu-cache1 libmowgli2 libmpg123-0 libnet-dbus-perl libnet-http-perl libnet-ssleay-perl libobrender27 libobt0 libonig2 liboobs-1-5 libopts25 libots0 libpisock9 libresid-builder0c2a libsidplay2 libsocket6-perl libtar0 libtidy-0.99-0 libtie-ixhash-perl libtimedate-perl libuniconf4.6 liburi-perl libvdpau1 libwebcam0 libwebkitgtk-1.0-0 libwebkitgtk-1.0-common libwv-1.2-4 libwvstreams4.6-base libwvstreams4.6-extras libwww-perl libwww-robotrules-perl libxfce4ui-1-0 libxfce4util-bin libxfce4util-common libxfce4util4 libxfconf-0-2 libxml-parser-perl libxml-twig-perl libxml-xpath-perl libxss1 lightdm-gtk-greeter link-grammar-dictionaries-en lm-sensors lp-solve lubuntu-artwork lubuntu-artwork-12-04 lubuntu-core lubuntu-default-settings lubuntu-desktop lubuntu-icon-theme lubuntu-software-center lxappearance lxappearance-obconf lxinput lxkeymap lxlauncher lxmenu-data lxpanel lxpanel-indicator-applet-plugin lxrandr lxsession lxsession-edit lxshortcut lxtask mplayer2 mtpaint ntp obconf openbox openbox-themes osmo pcmanfm pidgin pidgin-data pidgin-libnotify pidgin-microblog plymouth-theme-lubuntu-logo plymouth-theme-lubuntu-text python-pysqlite2 python-xklavier scrot sylpheed sylpheed-doc sylpheed-i18n sylpheed-plugins system-tools-backends transmission ttf-lyx uvcdynctrl uvcdynctrl-data wvdial xfburn xfce-keyboard-shortcuts xfce4-power-manager xfce4-power-manager-data xfconf xfonts-100dpi xpad xscreensaver xscreensaver-data && sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop && sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g unity-greeter && sudo update-initramfs -u

```

Things of particular note are:

1: I deliberately did NOT remove 'lxterminal', 'linux-headers-<version_number>-generic', and 'linux-headers-generic'. The reasoning behind not removing the 'linux-headers' packages is apparent, I questioned whether or not to remove lxterminal but decided not to.

2: Now we get into the 'lightdm' thing. Since Lubuntu now uses 'lightdm' rather than 'lxdm' things are a bit different. Lubuntu uses 'lightdm-gtk-greeter' but Ubuntu uses 'unity-greeter' and this gave me a few fits. Even if I left 'lightdm-gtk-greeter' installed X would choke on me.

Reinstalling 'lubuntu-core' did work fairly well but I didn't like the extra cruft so instead I added "sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g unity-greeter" which changes the default greeter.

Note: I haven't checked yet to see what the other flavors are using for dm's or, if using lightdm, which greeter they use but wanted to share both of these commands with you:

To select 'lightdm-gtk-greeter':

```
sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g lightdm-gtk-greeter

```

To select 'unity-greeter':

```
sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g unity-greeter
```

3: Plymouth is not a huge deal but I decided to deliberately remove 'plymouth-theme-lubuntu-logo' and 'plymouth-theme-lubuntu-text'. Since that leaves only the default Ubuntu plymouth theme just running "sudo update-initramfs -u" will take care of plymouth. No idea what to expect switching to other DE's yet but I'm sure you've dealt with the plymouth theme issue before.

Of course if more than one theme is installed you can change the default by running:

```
sudo update-alternatives --config default.plymouth
```

Followed by:

```
sudo update-initramfs -u
```

I'm certainly not suggesting that my way is the right way but thought I'd share what I've learned so far :D

I'll probably be tied up a great deal of the weekend.

---

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-04-28

All: any milage in a CLI install and a script? Either from alternate cd or from netinstall. The script asks questions then runs the alt-get commands....

Did you see these:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1959675](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1959675)

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11847760&postcount=103](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11847760&postcount=103)

During final iso-testing I did perform a CLI install with the Lubuntu alternate image which I assume provides a bit better wireless support and then just installed 'ubuntu-desktop' which worked just fine but Canonical's servers were incredibly slow at that time.

Anyway check out those posts and the internal links to see if any additional info is really needed. Something I need to double check is whether I'm right about Edubuntu and Mythbuntu using the pae kernel.

Sooooo many irons in the fire :)

NOTE: I can't be sure about the alternate images providing better wireless support than the netboot image because I run only wired desktops.

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-04-30
I just had time to do an Xubuntu Precise to Ubuntu Precise conversion:

```
sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop
```

Reboot at least twice just to be on the safe side, don't worry about the plymouth boot screen or the login screen at this point, just be sure you can reboot successfully at least twice. Next comes removing Xubuntu and reconfiguring the Ubuntu DE defaults:

```
sudo apt-get remove abiword abiword-common abiword-plugin-grammar abiword-plugin-mathview alacarte bison blueman brltty-x11 catfish exo-utils flex fonts-droid gigolo gimp gimp-data gmusicbrowser gnome-desktop-data gnome-system-tools gnome-time-admin gnumeric gnumeric-common gnumeric-doc gstreamer0.10-gnomevfs gthumb gthumb-data gtk2-engines-pixbuf indicator-application-gtk2 indicator-messages-gtk2 indicator-sound-gtk2 indicator-status-provider-pidgin leafpad libabiword-2.9 libao-common libao4 libaudio-scrobbler-perl libbabl-0.0-0 libbison-dev libcolamd2.7.1 libconfig-inifiles-perl libdigest-crc-perl libencode-locale-perl libexo-1-0 libexo-common libexo-helpers libfile-listing-perl libfl-dev libfont-afm-perl libgarcon-1-0 libgarcon-common libgdome2-0 libgdome2-cpp-smart0c2a libgegl-0.0-0 libgimp2.0 libglade2-0 libgnomevfs2-0 libgnomevfs2-common libgnomevfs2-extra libgoffice-0.8-8 libgoffice-0.8-8-common libgsf-1-114 libgsf-1-common libgstreamer-perl libgtk2-notify-perl libgtk2-trayicon-perl libgtkmathview0c2a libgtkspell0 libhtml-form-perl libhtml-format-perl libhtml-parser-perl libhtml-tagset-perl libhtml-tree-perl libhttp-cookies-perl libhttp-daemon-perl libhttp-date-perl libhttp-message-perl libhttp-negotiate-perl libid3tag0 libido-0.1-0 libilmbase6 libio-socket-inet6-perl libio-socket-ssl-perl libjavascriptcoregtk-1.0-0 libjpeg-progs libjpeg-turbo-progs libkeybinder0 liblaunchpad-integration1 liblink-grammar4 libloudmouth1-0 liblwp-mediatypes-perl liblwp-protocol-https-perl libmailtools-perl libnet-dbus-perl libnet-http-perl libnet-ssleay-perl liboobs-1-5 libopenexr6 libotr2 libots0 libsexy2 libsocket6-perl libtagc0 libthunarx-2-0 libtidy-0.99-0 libtie-ixhash-perl libtimedate-perl libtumbler-1-0 libunique-1.0-0 liburi-perl libwebkitgtk-1.0-0 libwebkitgtk-1.0-common libwv-1.2-4 libwww-perl libwww-robotrules-perl libxfce4ui-1-0 libxfce4util-bin libxfce4util-common libxfce4util4 libxfcegui4-4 libxfconf-0-2 libxml-parser-perl libxml-twig-perl libxml-xpath-perl libxss1 lightdm-gtk-greeter link-grammar-dictionaries-en lp-solve m4 mpg321 orage parole pastebinit pavucontrol pidgin pidgin-data pidgin-libnotify pidgin-microblog pidgin-otr python-configobj python-glade2 python-gmenu ristretto screensaver-default-images shimmer-themes system-tools-backends tcl8.5 thunar thunar-archive-plugin thunar-data thunar-media-tags-plugin thunar-volman ttf-droid ttf-lyx tumbler tumbler-common xchat xchat-common xfburn xfce-keyboard-shortcuts xfce4-appfinder xfce4-cpugraph-plugin xfce4-datetime-plugin xfce4-dict xfce4-indicator-plugin xfce4-mailwatch-plugin xfce4-netload-plugin xfce4-notes xfce4-notes-plugin xfce4-notifyd xfce4-panel xfce4-places-plugin xfce4-power-manager xfce4-power-manager-data xfce4-quicklauncher-plugin xfce4-screenshooter xfce4-session xfce4-settings xfce4-systemload-plugin xfce4-taskmanager xfce4-utils xfce4-verve-plugin xfce4-volumed xfce4-weather-plugin xfce4-xkb-plugin xfconf xfdesktop4 xfdesktop4-data xfwm4 xscreensaver xscreensaver-data xscreensaver-gl xubuntu-artwork xubuntu-default-settings xubuntu-desktop xubuntu-docs xubuntu-icon-theme xubuntu-wallpapers && sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop && sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g unity-greeter && sudo update-initramfs -u
```

It worked OK but I need to rinse & repeat using that copy-n-pasted command just in case ;)

I followed the same general guidelines as I did with converting Lubuntu to Ubuntu.

Kubuntu -> Ubuntu will be different because Kubuntu appears to still use kdm instead of lightdm so I'm in hopes that I'll get a debconf window asking me which display manager to use :)

EDIT: I repeated this process and all is well but just as with the Lubuntu -> Ubuntu I did NOT remove the native OS's terminal, and just to check I actually used xfce4-terminal to complete this. That may prove to be very important with Xubuntu because it's terminal does display in the Unity dashboard exactly the same as gnome-terminal!

So someone could easily choose the wrong terminal. What happens if you run a command in a terminal that you've chosen to remove? I don't know but I'm guessing it may be unpleasant.

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-04-30
> **aysiu said:**
> kansasnoob, you haven't been using my old "pure" tutorials on *buntu 12.04, have you?

I'm going to update them for 12.04 soon, and the package list definitely varies from release to release.

No, I've been cooking my own. My original concern was just the pae vs non-pae kernel issue which turns out to be easy to deal with - just never remove any kernel packages.

But it appears that all but Kubuntu now use lightdm as a display manager, but the use of different greeters can be problematic.

I was only sharing what I'd learned so far in hopes that it may be helpful to you in the near future :)

What the heck?

EDIT: See here:
[http://www.debian.org/doc/manuals/debian-faq/ch-pkg_basics.en.html#s-virtual](http://www.debian.org/doc/manuals/debian-faq/ch-pkg_basics.en.html#s-virtual)

EDIT2: On the offchance of aptitude doing any better than apt-get, I installed it and ran the whole string listed in your tutorial and all that accomplished was removing KDE (and a whole boatload of other stuff) entirely.

I've attached a text file of the output I got.

Note: KDE no longer loads after running that and it looks like all the gnome stuff is still there, judging from what is listed in /etc/init.d/

EDIT since I didn't mention it last night, removing all those packages from aptitude resulted in an unbootable system - all it did was sit at the kde start screen and didn't do anything else.

I will try it on the VM I was working on yesterday when I get home.

Snapshots = awesome. ;-)

### Post by CharlesA on 2012-05-03

EDIT: Xubuntu one worked when I just installed xubuntu-desktop, dunno if it'll be different if you just install it off the livecd.

### Post by aysiu on 2012-05-03
Thanks for testing. I really appreciate it. I've updated all the tutorials accordingly.

### Post by mips on 2012-05-03
Would be nice to add a minimal base install to that so people have a reference point for stripping any of the flavours down and doing their own thing.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2012-05-03
> **mips said:**
> Would be nice to add a minimal base install to that so people have a reference point for stripping any of the flavours down and doing their own thing.
That's why I created this:
[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/minimal](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/minimal)

I think you can, however, just uninstall *xorg* and *libgtk-3-0* or some other essential part, and everything else should come down with it.

---

### Post by mips on 2012-05-04
> **aysiu said:**
> That's why I created this:
[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/minimal](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/minimal)

I think you can, however, just uninstall *xorg* and *libgtk-3-0* or some other essential part, and everything else should come down with it.

Thanks aysiu, dunno how I missed that.

python-support python-xklavier rarian-compat scrot sgml-data sylpheed sylpheed-doc sylpheed-i18n sylpheed-plugins synaptic system-tools-backends transmission tsconf ttf-lyx uvcdynctrl uvcdynctrl-data wvdial xfburn xfce-keyboard-shortcuts xfce4-power-manager xfce4-power-manager-data xfconf xfonts-100dpi xpad xscreensaver xscreensaver-data && sudo apt-get install ubuntu-desktop
```

Ubuntu, remove Kubuntu works well here :)

Very specifically I installed and updated Kubuntu 12.04, I then ran "apt-get install ubuntu-desktop". During the installation dpkg asked which display manager I wanted to use and, while I knew that I'd be wise to choose 'lightdm', I chose the default 'kdm'. No real problem as it turns out :)

After installing 'ubuntu-desktop' I did boot both Kubuntu and Ubuntu twice just to be sure all was well, and it was. So then I ran your "Ubuntu, remove Kubuntu" script and all is well, but I should point out that I got this debconf choice:

[ATTACH]217277[/ATTACH]

Now, I would think the warning there is sufficient for anyone to choose the default "no". What do you think?

Anyway I stuck with the default "no" and all is well.

Upon completion I was told:

```
The following packages were automatically installed and are no longer required:
  libopenal1 libts-0.0-0 libmodplug1 libwildmidi1 libzbar0 libflite1
  libdc1394-22 libxvidcore4 libwildmidi-config libzvbi0 libdca0 libopenspc0
  libfftw3-3 libslv2-9 libass4 libvo-aacenc0 libdirectfb-1.2-9 libenca0
  libvo-amrwbenc0 libsoundtouch0 libzvbi-common libgme0 libspandsp2 freepats
  libgstreamer-plugins-bad0.10-0 libkate1 libcdaudio1 libmimic0 tsconf
  libopenal-data libcelt0-0 libdirac-encoder0 libofa0 libmms0 libfaad2
Use 'apt-get autoremove' to remove them.

```

But I ignored that and continued. Certainly running "apt-get -f install" shows the same thing and choosing to "autoremove" results in the removal of very little "bloat"

```
lance@lance-VIA-desktop:~$ sudo apt-get autoremove
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree       
Reading state information... Done
The following packages will be REMOVED:
  freepats libass4 libcdaudio1 libcelt0-0 libdc1394-22 libdca0
  libdirac-encoder0 libdirectfb-1.2-9 libenca0 libfaad2 libfftw3-3 libflite1
  libgme0 libgstreamer-plugins-bad0.10-0 libkate1 libmimic0 libmms0
  libmodplug1 libofa0 libopenal-data libopenal1 libopenspc0 libslv2-9
  libsoundtouch0 libspandsp2 libts-0.0-0 libvo-aacenc0 libvo-amrwbenc0
  libwildmidi-config libwildmidi1 libxvidcore4 libzbar0 libzvbi-common
  libzvbi0 tsconf
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 35 to remove and 0 not upgraded.
After this operation, 68.0 MB disk space will be freed.

```

So I think your "pure Ubuntu - remove Kubuntu" is OK :D

Now I'll try to go the other way :guitar:

---

### Post by CharlesA on 2012-05-04
Thanks for confirming it, kansasnoob.

I picked kde when I installed kubuntu-desktop and it removed it fine as well.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2012-05-04
Thank you both for testing.

---

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-05-04
> **aysiu said:**
> Thank you both for testing.

We're not out of the woods just yet :(

Your same "pure Ubuntu-remove Lubuntu/Xubuntu" commands are likely to fail on reboot due to removing 'linux-headers-3.2.0-24', 'linux-headers-3.2.0-24-generic', and 'linux-headers-generic' but I'm going to give it a try :)

And I really think it would be best to include a command to reset the default lightdm greeter, but we need to go one step at a time.

It's also possible that 32 bit will differ from 64 bit ATM. Luckily for these purposes 12.04 is the end of the road altogether for the non-pae kernel so things will get simpler again in 12.10 :)

---

### Post by CharlesA on 2012-05-04
Xubuntu worked for me on 64-bit after rebooting. The only thing the headers are used for are compiling aren't they?

---

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-05-04
> **CharlesA said:**
> Xubuntu worked for me on 64-bit after rebooting. The only thing the headers are used for are compiling aren't they?

I'm not sure. I originally got into this because of the pae vs non-pae issue. My concern was that I've often recommended Aysiu's tutorials and I wanted to make her aware of any potential issues.

I've written just a few tutorials, two examples:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1886799](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1886799)

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1966370](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1966370)

And I'm working on this one:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1959675](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1959675)

Ultimately I'm only trying to be helpful :)

---

### Post by CharlesA on 2012-05-04
Thanks for the links. :)

More info is always a good thing.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2012-05-04
As long as you don't remove *linux-headers-generic* and *linux-headers-generic-pae*, you should be fine... I think.

---

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-05-04
> **aysiu said:**
> As long as you don't remove *linux-headers-generic* and *linux-headers-generic-pae*, you should be fine... I think.

That is my belief ATM, but I think your "remove Lubuntu/Xubuntu" scripts in post #554 do remove those don't they?

But I also want to try them just as is, and it would be interesting to see just how it works with 64 bit. If CharlesA is correct then nothing should be effected, although we need to also think how future updates may be effected.

I need to lay down for a while ............. getting old, both mind and body giving out :cry:

### Post by CharlesA on 2012-05-04
If there is a new kernel update, new headers are installed, and that would pull in any dependencies that are missing. ;)

Hope you feel better, kansasnoob.

OK I just tried the Ubuntu, remove Xubuntu script as posted here and I encountered the expected lightdm problem but I'll do this in the order of occurrence. I began by installing and updating Xubuntu Precise, and then just ran "apt-get install ubuntu-desktop".

After being certain that both Xubuntu and Ubuntu booted OK I opened the dashboard and typed term:

[ATTACH]217314[/ATTACH]

As you can see there both the 'gnome-terminal' and 'xfce4-terminal' are largely indistinguishable in the dash. That's what worries me about removing any OS's native terminal. Being familiar with the global-menu I just clicked on Help > About to be sure I had 'gnome-terminal' open and then ran your script.

When the script was done running I rebooted and was, quite expectedly, presented with the following while trying to boot up:

[ATTACH]217315[/ATTACH]

That's one of the things I was trying to explain clear back in post #518. AFAIK the entire Ubuntu family uses 'lightdm' now except for Kubuntu which still uses 'kdm', but Ubuntu itself uses 'unity-greeter' and the rest of the tribe uses 'lightdm-gtk-greeter', and just installing or reinstalling DE's does not change the greeter used so you must include the proper one of these:

To select 'lightdm-gtk-greeter':

```
sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g lightdm-gtk-greeter
```

To select 'unity-greeter':

```
sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g unity-greeter
```

Anyway I was able to fix things by booting into recovery mode and typing "/usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g unity-greeter". 

Totally off-topic but I should also mention that I was surprised selecting "root" in recovery mode mounts files and folders as read only so I first had to enable networking and then select root again to get read & write capability. That may effect other uses of the recovery mode :(

After that I was able to boot normally though and the only indication that removing those kernel header packages may be any kind of a problem is this:

[ATTACH]217316[/ATTACH]

But I need to take a peak at some other conversions where I specifically did not remove any kernel packages for a comparison.

I actually got to thinking that it may be wise if we'd create these scripts using 64 bit OS's to eliminate the kernel mess anyway, and I've sort of been wanting to try 64 bit on my Intel Atom anyway, but it may take me a few days to get it done.

---

### Post by Paddy Landau on 2012-05-09
> **kansasnoob said:**
> Totally off-topic but I should also mention that I was surprised selecting "root" in recovery mode mounts files and folders as read only so I first had to enable networking and then select root again to get read & write capability. That may effect other uses of the recovery mode :(
It certainly does affect other uses of the recovery mode. See [my recent thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1975474") about it, and [the bug since raised]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/996454") (you may want to vote for the bug).

---

I subscribed too so I'll try to follow up ;)

### Post by aysiu on 2012-05-09
> **kansasnoob said:**
> 
As you can see there both the 'gnome-terminal' and 'xfce4-terminal' are largely indistinguishable in the dash. That's what worries me about removing any OS's native terminal. Being familiar with the global-menu I just clicked on Help > About to be sure I had 'gnome-terminal' open and then ran your script. I knew this to be true, but I just tested it, and you can run removal commands from the very terminal you're removing. I just removed Kubuntu from Ubuntu using the Konsole terminal, and one of the programs I was removing was Konsole.

> 
That's one of the things I was trying to explain clear back in post #518. AFAIK the entire Ubuntu family uses 'lightdm' now except for Kubuntu which still uses 'kdm', but Ubuntu itself uses 'unity-greeter' and the rest of the tribe uses 'lightdm-gtk-greeter', and just installing or reinstalling DE's does not change the greeter used so you must include the proper one of these:

To select 'lightdm-gtk-greeter':

```
sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g lightdm-gtk-greeter
```

To select 'unity-greeter':

```
sudo /usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g unity-greeter
```

Anyway I was able to fix things by booting into recovery mode and typing "/usr/lib/lightdm/lightdm-set-defaults -g unity-greeter".  I'm going to do some more testing on this.

> I actually got to thinking that it may be wise if we'd create these scripts using 64 bit OS's to eliminate the kernel mess anyway, and I've sort of been wanting to try 64 bit on my Intel Atom anyway, but it may take me a few days to get it done. I did a removal of the kernel stuff, and everything booted up okay. I don't know why it worked, but it did.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2012-05-09
I've also confirmed you can remove Ubuntu from Xubuntu using the Gnome terminal, but you're totally right about the *lightdm* thing. I'll modify the tutorials accordingly. Thanks for the legwork.

### Post by aysiu on 2012-05-10
> **Shadius said:**
> Before I was directed to your tutorial, I was recommended to use Gnome Tweak Tool to get GNOME Classic. I was just curious to know what the differences between the method described in your tutorial and the Gnome Tweak Tool were. Also, I thought maybe you'd want to include it in your tutorial as a second method? Only a suggestion. Thanks. :)
Gnome Tweak Tool is for tweaking Gnome 3.

Gnome Classic is actually Gnome 2.

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-05-10
Not sure if these would be helpful to you but I had just completed installing the amd64 versions of Ubuntu, Lubuntu, Xubuntu, and Kubuntu - then running "apt-get install" with the other flavors to gather info. [ATTACH]217621[/ATTACH]

About the lightdm greeters, I was wrong to say that only Ubuntu uses 'unity-greeter'. It turns out that Edubuntu also uses 'unity-greeter', but all except Ubuntu and Edubuntu use 'lightdm-gtk-greeter'. Just wanted to be factually correct even though I doubt we'd want or need conversions for Edubuntu, Mythbuntu, or Ubuntu Studio.

### Post by kansasnoob on 2012-05-10
> **aysiu said:**
> Gnome Tweak Tool is for tweaking Gnome 3.

Gnome Classic is actually Gnome 2.

Actually, in both Oneiric and Precise, once you've installed 'gnome-session-fallback' and you log into either Gnome Classic or Gnome Classic (no effects) you are running Gnome 3 in what Gnome calls "fallback mode". I maintain about 40 PC's for fellow seniors and most of them don't like Unity so I've been working on this:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1966370](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1966370)

It's not yet as complete as the Oneiric version but there is no huge rush as I won't start upgrading them from Lucid until 12.04.1 is released. I love that 12.04 is a five year LTS :)

I generally avoid 'gnome-tweak-tool' because it also pulls in 'gnome-shell' which I won't use. I always prefer installing the bare minimum additional packages.

---

### Post by Adrian98 on 2012-05-10
> **aysiu said:**
> **For New Users:**
[The Psychocats site](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/) is a great site for new users to explore. It uses a combination of terminal commands and screenshot-heavy point-and-click tutorials to help get you up and running with Ubuntu, and it addresses many common questions and problems newcomers have when starting with Ubuntu.

Psychocats isn't intended to compete with other documentation sites. Its goal is to give a uniform look and feel for several key tutorials and, more importantly, not to overwhelm or confuse new users with too many tutorials in hard-to-find places. Each tutorial addresses a common Ubuntu question, and all the internal links are on the sidebar.

If you're interested in a more comprehensive set of tutorials, especially ones aimed at advanced or intermediate use (or just anything not covered on my site--for example, how to use *ndiswrapper*), you may want to check out [the Tutorials & Tips section of these forums,](http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=100) or [the official community-maintained documentation](https://help.ubuntu.com/community).

**For Other Users:**
Thank you for all your help so far!

Users on these forums have caught typos and other errors and kept me abreast of changes in versions of software I don't use (e.g. Songbird). Some users have even contributed whole paragraphs to the Psychocats tutorials, and I've tried to credit them appropriately.

**Working with Psychocats:**
I've had numerous requests asking if people can link to or translate the Psychocats Ubuntu website. The answer is *yes*. You can link to Psychocats without asking my permission. If you want to translate Psychocats Ubuntu, you may do that as well (please let me know, though--I'm just curious to know what's going on), and I encourage spreading the knowledge. I haven't officially licensed the documentation, but the closest I've found to what I'd say embodies the spirit with which I'm giving Psychocats Ubuntu to the community is [the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 license ](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/3.0/). If you would like to mirror Psychocats, you may do so, but please send me a link to the mirrored site, so I can know to refer people there also.

**Future Updates and Feedback:**
If you have any constructive criticism, please leave it in this thread. I'll be checking back from time to time. I devote a lot of time to the Ubuntu community and to these forums, especially, but this is not my main job. So go ahead and leave any suggestions you may have, but I may not get around to implementing them right away (or at all, depending on how good the suggestion is).

If the Psychocats website has helped you, you can post that here, too. It's always good to know what people have found helpful, and which pages are most frequently used by people.

Thanks aysiu... the Tutorials are damn useful for me! As being a newbie in Ubuntu usage, i need more and more commands to learn! Thanks a lot , indeed helpful!

### Post by %rm on 2012-05-24
Hello,
the Psychocats [backup tutorial]("http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/backup") explains rsync and tar usage nicely. I am using rsync for both personal and system backup, or to be more accurate, my versions of the system configuration files. I don't bother backing up complete system including the Lubuntu packages which can easily be recovered from the Internet. Hence I rsync /home, /etc and all version control directories (rcs, git, bzr) every hour from /etc/crontab, which has been more than enough so far.

Psychocat pages were great help in changing from Ubuntu to Lubuntu and LXDE. Thanks!

    BR, Risto


/etc/crontab:

```
42 *	* * *	root	nice /usr/local/sbin/backup >> /var/log/backup.log
```

The script:

```
#!/bin/bash

# Backup to My Book RAID disk

date
DST="/media/MyBook/paja-backup"
SRC=`locate -b '\RCS'`
SRC="$SRC `locate -b '\.git'`"
SRC="$SRC /home /etc"
echo $SRC

for d in $SRC
do
    CMD="rsync -ah ${d}/ $DST/${d:1}"
    $CMD
done
```

### Post by Fernhill Linux Project on 2012-06-08
RE : Fix Broken Sudo changes in 12.04

I just changed our guides, after running through this process again, it seems that from 12.04 the 'admin' group is now called 'sudo'. You now need to use ```
adduser username sudo
``` 

Hope it helps & thanks for your help.
;)

[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/PrecisePangolin/TechnicalOverview/Alpha2#General]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/PrecisePangolin/TechnicalOverview/Alpha2#General")

### Post by Joeb on 2012-07-04
I've run into an issue going from Ubuntu 12.04 to Kubuntu 12.04 using the pure kubuntu method on psychocats.net.

Specifically, it appears to work fine, however there are intermittant lock ups and it is fairly laggy.  On the otherhand, doing a clean install of kubuntu does not have this problem. I also do not have a problem going from kubuntu to ubuntu using the pure ubuntu method.

I haven't been able to figure it out, yet, but it seems that some settings on Ubuntu aren't cleared and are conflicting with Kubuntu.

I have repeated this process several times just to make sure it wasn't a fluke and each time, the converted Ubuntu to Kubuntu system manifests the problem.

Just thought you might want to know.

### Post by aysiu on 2012-07-04
Yeah, when you remove Ubuntu using "PureKubuntu," all you're really doing is removing the software packages and not the user settings. That said, in theory the user settings for uninstalled programs should not affect installed programs... at least not for any reason I can think of.

Hi aysiu (& kansasnoob)

I'd love to run Edubuntu with XFCE instead of Gnome.  In other words, all Edubuntu's education-oriented packages plus my favourite lightweight desktop (without duplicated apps from Xubuntu).

The PureXYZ scripts come close to doing this but not quite.  Would it be possible to create a tutorial for picking [favourite-distro] + [favourite-desktop]?

For my example (Edubuntu + XFCE) I can imagine:

 1. Install Ubuntu.
 2. Make a list of packages in Edubuntu that are not in Ubuntu:
    apt-get --dry-run install edubuntu-desktop
 3. Remove edubuntu-desktop and any packages with "gnome" in their names from the list.
 4. Install Xubuntu.
 5. Add the Edubuntu packages (without instantiating the Gnome DE):
    apt-get install <package-list>

I might still get some unwanted Xubuntu packages though.  I also don't know about display manager/greeter issues?

What do you think? :)

### Post by Paddy Landau on 2012-08-13
> **schooner said:**
> 3. Remove edubuntu-desktop and any packages with "gnome" in their names from the list.
 4. Install Xubuntu.
You should be able to to it much more easily than that.

Simply install [FONT=Lucida Console]xubuntu-desktop[/FONT]. When you next log in, you will have a choice of using the LXDE login instead of your normal one. The login screen remembers your choice, so you won't have to choose it every time you log in.

If you don't like LXDE, you can revert to your previous login method at the login screen.

---

### Post by schooner on 2012-08-13
> **Paddy Landau said:**
> 
Simply install [FONT=Lucida Console]xubuntu-desktop[/FONT]. When you next log in, you will have a choice of using the LXDE login instead of your normal one. The login screen remembers your choice, so you won't have to choose it every time you log in.


Yes, I know about desktop switching, I've tried it. :)

The problem with adding a DE to an existing installation is that you get all the new desktop's preferred applications for various functions (word processing, DVD burners, movie players, etc.).

The menus get cluttered with two terminals, two ways of listening to music, etc.  I'd like to keep it simple for the kids with edubuntu.

Hence my elaborate attempt above to keep only the edubuntu apps, and substitute XFCE for Gnome.  I don't want Xubuntu's abiword etc.

Reading back through this thread I see plenty of expertise with the "PureXYZ" tutorials.  Anyone like to suggest a tutorial method for choosing [favourite-app-collection] + [favourite-desktop] independently from the *buntu suite of distros?  In my case edubutu's apps + XFCE. :)

### Post by oldfred on 2012-08-13
Someone posted this:

XFCE installs XFCE only while Xubuntu-desktop all other programmes
sudo apt-get install xfce4 xfce4-goodies

Comparison of desktop environments 
[https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Desktop_Environment](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Desktop_Environment)
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_X_Window_System_desktop_environments](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_X_Window_System_desktop_environments)

search for packages all distributions:
[http://packages.ubuntu.com/search?keywords=ume-config-common](http://packages.ubuntu.com/search?keywords=ume-config-common)

List of default applications:
Look for manifests for version:
[http://releases.ubuntu.com/precise/](http://releases.ubuntu.com/precise/)

Then to install that manifest:
dpkg-query -W --showformat='${Package} ${Version}\n' > casper/filesystem.manifest
apt-get install -y $(awk '{print $1}' filesystem.manifest)
Look for manifests for version:
[http://releases.ubuntu.com/precise/](http://releases.ubuntu.com/precise/)

### Post by ytkuser12 on 2012-11-13
hey all i was going to use the virtual box thing and newest ubuntu i was curious how much ram to give it and how much video memory to give it. also how many processors do i want to let it have. i want the least trouble with it as possible. i have GTX 555 video card and a  i7-3770 CPU. and 8 gigs ram i like the idea of runing a operating system inside a virtual machine sounds like fun.

---

### Post by Paddy Landau on 2012-11-14
> **ytkuser12 said:**
> … i was curious how much ram to give it and how much video memory to give it. also how many processors do i want to let it have.
This post probably belongs in a new thread in the [Virtualisation forum]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=308"). However, a quick answer:

To run Ubuntu 64-bit successfully in VB, you only need 2Gb RAM, and two processors (well, you can give it only one, but two works better). Mine works well with 50Mb video memory. Whatever you choose, you **can** change your mind later; VB will allow you to change the settings any time while the virtual machine is powered off.

Remember that while the virtual machine is running, whatever resources you allocate to your virtual machine are unavailable to your host.

If you have any problems or further questions, please open a new thread in the [Virtualisation forum]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=308").

### Post by HunterZ on 2013-06-16
> **aysiu said:**
> I know people weren't big fans of the last time I switched Psychocats over to a blog format, but the static pages are a bit difficult to maintain, and I think ultimately search engines glom onto relevant content, so for Raring Ringtail, I'll be switching back to a blog format for the Psychocats tutorials. Stay tuned! And thanks in advance for your patience and understanding. Any change can be frustrating and take some getting used to.
You should consider using a wiki page that only you have access to edit. This has a host of advantages over a blog format, including:

[LIST]
[*]Allows you to keep a history of changes to pages, and compare revisions 
[*]Allows people to follow changes via RSS (blogs support RSS, but don't add a new entry to the feed when you just modify an existing article) 
[*]Extremely easy to make edits via web-based interface 
[*]Article URLs are predictable, and attractive to search engines 
[/LIST]
 
> **aysiu said:**
> It may take me a couple of weeks to update, but  I'm downloading Raring via torrent at home (I'm at work now), so thanks  in advance for your patience (those of you who actually look at my  tutorials).
Thanks, I'm looking forward to this. I was thinking of trying some different desktop environments on one of my Linux boxes again, but was disappointed to see that your info hasn't yet been updated for Raring. Your site has been very helpful over the years :)

### Post by aysiu on 2013-06-17
Yeah, my updating Raring plans kind of fell through... at least a comprehensive update. Truth be told, I may not do many updates at all in the future. I had grand plans to keep this up indefinitely, but things have been getting busier at work, and I've honestly been using Ubuntu less and less. If you're "dog fooding" and using Ubuntu every day, it's a lot easier to keep up up-to-date and relevant tutorials. Most of my time these days is in Windows, Mac, Android, and ChromeOS. I'm also trying to teach myself PHP and MySQL (to use at work but also for personal enrichment). Ubuntu desktop has fallen a bit by the wayside for me. It is possible that if Ubuntu Touch gets off the ground in a real way, and I can flash it and use it every day on my Nexus phone and Nexus tablet that I'll revive the tutorials to be Ubuntually relevant. Time will tell. I'm glad the tutorials have been useful for the past eight years. Maybe they will be useful again one day.

---

### Post by newb85 on 2013-09-22
I don't think psychocats is still actively writing tutorials. If you can't find detailed enough instructions in existing threads or resources, your best bet is probably to start your own thread with a descriptive title of what you're looking for. 

I do think there are plenty of resources available, though I haven't personally installed alongside Windows 8.  Install from live DVD or USB, making sure to use the same partition scheme as Windows.
